Section 2
GenAI vs Classical ML
Click any row to see a concrete enterprise example
DIMENSION
CLASSICAL ML
GENERATIVE AI
The Four Eras — click each to compare
How it learnsHumans hand-code every if-then rule. No learning from data.
LimitationBreaks on anything outside the rulebook. Can't generalise.
ExamplesTax calculators, ELIZA (1966), MYCIN medical diagnosis (1970s)
Why replacedReal world has too many exceptions to codify by hand
How it learnsLearns statistical patterns from labelled training data
LimitationNeeds hand-crafted features; struggles with raw images/audio/text
ExamplesSpam filters, credit scoring, fraud detection, recommendation engines
Why replacedFeature engineering is expensive and doesn't scale to unstructured data
How it learnsLearns features automatically from raw data via deep neural networks
LimitationNeeds massive labelled datasets; expensive GPU training; output is a prediction not new content
ExamplesImage recognition (AlexNet 2012), speech-to-text, AlphaGo
Why replacedStill discriminative — classifies input, doesn't create output. Can't write, draw, or code.
How it learnsSelf-supervised pre-training on internet-scale unlabelled data (predict next token)
StrengthOne model, many tasks. Generates novel content. Natural language interface — no code needed.
ExamplesGPT-4, Claude, Gemini, DALL-E, Sora, Suno, Stable Diffusion
Enterprise implicationDemocratises AI — a business analyst can now use AI without a data science team
Before the game — two words you need: A Discriminative model looks at an input and picks a label or a decision from a fixed set of options — "is this spam?", "is this a cat?", "will this loan default?". A Generative model creates brand-new content that didn't exist before — an email, an image, a line of code. Classical ML (the left column above) is almost always discriminative; GenAI (the right column) is generative. That's the entire game below.
In-class game 1 of 3 Discriminative vs Generative Sort
›
⏱ 10–12 minutes · teaches: core AI paradigm distinction

Trainer reads 10 AI tool descriptions. Students respond silently with a hand signal — no shouting. Click each item on screen to reveal the correct answer after votes are in.

  1. Trainer reads each item below (question only — answer is hidden until you click it)
  2. Students respond silently: raise BOTH hands for DISCRIMINATIVE, raise your RIGHT hand only for GENERATIVE
  3. Trainer scans the room, then clicks the item below to reveal the correct answer — compare against the room's hands
  4. Wrap: "Everything you've used casually for years is mostly discriminative. The new wave is generative."
🖐️🖐️ Both hands = Discriminative  ·  🖐️ Right hand only = Generative  ·  Click each row below to reveal
  • Gmail spam filter→ Discriminative
  • ChatGPT writing a cover letter→ Generative
  • Netflix "you might also like"→ Discriminative
  • DALL-E illustrating a futuristic Samsung campus→ Generative
  • Google Photos identifying faces→ Discriminative
  • GitHub Copilot completing your code→ Generative
  • Credit scoring model predicting loan default→ Discriminative
  • Suno composing a song from a text prompt→ Generative
  • Medical AI flagging a tumour on an X-ray→ Discriminative
  • Samsung Gauss summarising a meeting transcript→ Generative
Section 3
The Road to the Agentic Era
2017 → 2026 · Click any event to expand · Colour-coded by company
🧠 Why did AlphaGo (2016) matter more than Deep Blue (1997) beating Kasparov at chess?
Deep Blue used brute-force computation + handcrafted rules — it was the Rule-Based era at scale. AlphaGo used deep reinforcement learning to develop intuition the programmers couldn't articulate. Go has more board positions than atoms in the universe — no rulebook could cover it. This proved deep learning could master domains where human-codified knowledge fails.
Section 4
2026 Model Landscape
Click a card to see when to use / when NOT to use · Filter by type
The brands you already know — click each to see enterprise fit
Before the technical model grid below — how do the consumer-facing products map to Samsung use cases?
🧠 Enterprise check: Samsung is building a tool to analyse 200-page supplier contracts for risk clauses. No data can leave Samsung's servers. Which model family fits — and which doesn't?
Open-weight models (Llama 3.3, Mistral) deployed on Samsung's own infrastructure. Cloud-hosted APIs (ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude) are ruled out — data cannot leave the perimeter. This is a common enterprise constraint you will encounter at every MNC client.
Section 5
How LLMs Work: The 5-Step Pipeline
Click NEXT to advance · Each step = one concept
Before the game — what "temperature" actually is: it's a number you set whenever you call a model — either in code (temperature: 0.7) or in a tool's settings/playground. It controls how far down the model's ranked list of next-word guesses it's allowed to reach.
  • 0 — always pick the single most likely word. Deterministic, same answer every time. Use for code, data extraction, factual Q&A.
  • ~1 — the default in most chat tools. Some natural variety, still mostly sensible.
  • Higher (up to 2 on some APIs) — reaches much further down the list. More creative, more surprising, more likely to go off the rails. Use for brainstorming, not for facts.
In the game below, the "HIGH TEMP" card-holders are physically playing temperature ≈ 1.5–2.
In-class game 2 of 3 Human Autocomplete
›
⏱ 10–12 minutes · teaches: next-token prediction, temperature, context window

Students become the LLM — each person adds ONE word and passes it on. Physical demonstration of autoregressive generation, mapping directly to the 5-step pipeline above. Designed for a large room: only 8–10 students actively play each round, everyone else watches and reacts.

Setup: Pull 8–10 volunteers into a line at the front — they are the model. Everyone else stays seated and watches. Someone writes each word on the whiteboard/projector as it's said, so the whole room can track the sentence forming. Starter: "The best way to learn machine learning is to…"
ROUND 1 — Next-token relay

Each person in line says ONE word, left to right, fast, no pauses. This is "predict the next word" — one forward pass per person.

ROUND 2 — Temperature (no shoulder-tapping — use secret cards)
  1. Before starting, secretly hand 2–3 people in the line a folded card: "When your turn comes, do NOT say the obvious next word. Say a surprising word instead — it must still make grammatical sense, just not what anyone would expect."
  2. Restart the relay from the same or a new starter sentence. Everyone else plays normally — says the obvious next word.
  3. When a card-holder's turn comes, they reveal nothing — they just say their surprising word (e.g. "practice" → "dinosaurs" or "spontaneously" instead of "with" or "real"). The next student has to keep the sentence going from wherever that left it.
  4. Debrief: "Where exactly did it get weird? Did the sentence recover or keep drifting? When would Samsung want temperature LOW — a support script, extracting a JSON field? When HIGH — brainstorming ad copy, a creative Galaxy AI wallpaper prompt?"
ROUND 3 — Context window limit (send 4 students outside)
  1. At the start of this round, quietly send 4 students outside — they must not hear anything said after this point
  2. The remaining line keeps adding words until the board has 10–12 words total
  3. Erase the first 5 words from the board, leaving only the later words visible
  4. Bring the 4 students back in. They can only see the truncated board — ask them to continue the sentence, or ask "what do you think this was originally about?" They can't know — they weren't there
  5. Debrief: "The context window is a fixed-size viewing window, not infinite memory — once full, the oldest content is evicted to make room for new content. Evicted content isn't fuzzy memory, it's completely inaccessible — exactly like the 4 students who were physically absent. What could go wrong in a Samsung Care+ chatbot if this happened mid-conversation and the customer's order number was in an evicted message?"

Attention: Q · K · V
2-MINUTE ANALOGY — THE COLLEGE LIBRARY

Imagine you're in the college library. You ask: "I need a book on Machine Learning." That request is the whole Q/K/V system in miniature — click each card below to see which part you just played.

Click each to understand what it does
Query = what I'm looking for  ·  Key = labels used for matching  ·  Value = actual information returned
Section 6b
Diffusion Models — how AI generates images
Completely different from Transformers. They learn to reverse noise.
Forward (training)Take a real image → add tiny amounts of Gaussian noise iteratively → after ~1000 steps: pure static. The model learns what noise was added at each step.
Reverse (generation)Start with pure random noise → iteratively denoise, guided by a text prompt (via CLIP/T5 text encoder), until a coherent image emerges after ~20–50 steps.
Latent diffusionModern models (Stable Diffusion, DALL-E 3) work in a compressed latent space — 64×64 instead of 512×512 pixels — then upscale at the end. This is why image generation takes seconds, not hours.
Transformer vs Diffusion — which to use when
Transformer LLMDiffusion Model
OutputText, code, structured data, audioImages, video, 3D assets
Generates viaAutoregressive token prediction (left→right)Iterative denoising from random noise
StrengthReasoning, language, instruction following, logicPhotorealism, artistic style, visual coherence
Enterprise modelsGPT-4.1, Claude, Gemini, Llama 3.3, MistralDALL-E 3, Stable Diffusion XL, Midjourney, Adobe Firefly
Samsung usesGalaxy AI text features, Bixby, Gauss LanguageGenerative Wallpaper (Galaxy S24+), Vision AI upscaling
🧠 A Samsung engineer needs to build two features: (1) a chatbot that answers Galaxy Watch troubleshooting questions, (2) an AI that upscales SD photos to 4K on Samsung TVs. Which model architecture fits each feature — and why are they different?
(1) Troubleshooting chatbot → Transformer-based LLM (decoder-only, like GPT/Claude). Needs to understand natural language questions and generate text responses. (2) 4K upscaling → a form of diffusion/generative upscaling model, or a super-resolution CNN — it needs to generate realistic pixel content, not text. Samsung Vision AI uses exactly this approach on their Neo QLED TVs.

Section 9
Embeddings: Meaning as Numbers
Hover over dots to see phrases · Similar meaning = closer position

What is an embedding?

A vector — a list of ~1536 numbers — that captures the meaning of text. Texts with similar meaning have vectors that are mathematically close in space. The scatter plot shows this: SQL query phrases cluster together, error messages cluster together — far from unrelated content.

ENTERPRISE USE CASES
🔍 Semantic search — "show revenue query" finds all revenue-related SQL
📚 RAG — retrieve relevant docs before calling the LLM (Day 4)
🔄 Duplicate detection — find near-identical pipeline configs
🏷️ Data catalogue — auto-tag tables by semantic similarity
COMMON EMBEDDING MODELS
text-embedding-3-smallOpenAI · 1536 dims · cloud
all-MiniLM-L6-v2HuggingFace · free · local
nomic-embed-textOllama · free · local · fast
In-class game 3 of 3 Human Embedding Space
›
⏱ 10–12 minutes · teaches: embeddings, semantic similarity, vector space

Students physically become the vector space — proximity in the room = semantic similarity. No maths required. Run this right before or after the scatter chart above.

  1. Clear a space. Trainer calls a word — students move to show similarity. People cluster near words they feel are related, move away from opposites.
  2. Round 1: King, Queen, Prince, Car, Bus, Dog, Cat, Apple, Orange, Samsung, Apple (the company) — watch "Apple" cluster differently depending on context
  3. Trainer calls "King minus Man plus Woman" — who do you stand near? (Answer: Queen)
  4. Round 2: Seoul, Tokyo, Samsung, Apple, Hunger, Tired, Happy, Fast, Slow
  5. Debrief: "This spatial relationship — similar meaning = nearby coordinates — is exactly what an embedding layer does inside every LLM, and exactly what the scatter chart above just showed you in real numbers."

Section 10
Hallucination: The #1 Enterprise AI Risk
Click each card to reveal a real hallucination pattern · Prevention tips included
Section 11
Bias in LLMs
4 bias types every AI practitioner should know · Click to expand
KEY INSIGHT

Bias is not a flaw — it is a characteristic of training data. You cannot eliminate it, but you can engineer around it: specify platform, version, locale, and context explicitly in every prompt.

Section 12
Responsible AI: 5 Core Pillars
Click any pillar to see the DE implication
⚠️ SAMSUNG GENAI PROGRAM POLICY

All AI-generated content — text, code, images, or structured data — requires human review before deployment or publication. "The AI generated it" is not an acceptable answer in a post-incident review.

⚖️ CASE STUDY — Air Canada (2024)

Air Canada's AI chatbot told a customer he could buy a full-price bereavement ticket and claim a retroactive discount later — a policy that didn't exist. The customer relied on it, paid full price, was denied the refund, and sued. The court ruled: Air Canada is responsible for what its chatbot says.

Enterprise implication: when AI speaks on behalf of your company, your company owns the consequences — legally and reputationally, covering both Copyright and Human Oversight risk. Every customer-facing AI deployment needs a human review gate for consequential outputs. This is the focus of Day 4's role-play exercise.

GenAI × Enterprise Opportunity Map

Section 13b
Samsung AI Ecosystem
The technology you just studied is already inside products used by 600 million Samsung device owners — click each to see the GenAI feature
SAMSUNG'S STRATEGIC BET

On-device AI — processing happens on the phone/TV/appliance itself, not in the cloud. This protects user privacy, reduces latency, and works offline. It requires extremely efficient models (small enough for an Exynos chip, powerful enough to be useful). This is a hard engineering problem and a major differentiator vs competitors who rely entirely on cloud APIs.

Section 13c
Prompt Engineering — Entry Point
The same model, the same question — two completely different outputs depending on how you ask. Enough to run today's labs; the full 6-element framework is Day 2.

Note: Chapter 2 (Day 2) is entirely dedicated to prompt engineering — 6 hours, the full 6-element framework, advanced techniques (Chain-of-Thought, Tree-of-Thought, ReAct). Today is just enough to run Lab 2 and Lab 3 effectively.

❌ WEAK PROMPT — TYPICAL FIRST ATTEMPT
Write something about Samsung.
✅ ENGINEERED PROMPT — 4 ELEMENTS APPLIED
You are a technology analyst writing for Samsung's investor relations team. Write a 3-sentence executive summary of Samsung's Galaxy AI strategy for the Q3 2025 earnings call. Focus on competitive differentiation vs Apple. Use formal business language. No bullet points — prose only.
4-element framework for today (full 6-element: Day 2)
ElementWhat to specifyExample
RoleWho should the AI act as?"You are a Samsung AI Systems Analyst…"
GoalWhat specific output do you need?"…evaluate Claude vs GPT-4 for enterprise deployment"
ContextRelevant background / constraints"Samsung processes sensitive user data; no cloud APIs allowed"
Output formatLength, structure, tone"3-bullet recommendation brief, formal business language"

Today's Labs
Your Mission Today
3 labs · Push once at the end of the day

Silver

🥇

Lab 1 · Not scored

Setup & First Run

Verify tools, fork the repo, push a test file — prove your pipeline works before anything is graded

Confirm you can log into ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini. Fork the trainer's repo, clone it, edit checkin.json, and push it. This confirms your git flow works before Lab 2 and Lab 3 depend on it — and shows you how the dashboard/badge system works.

  • Verify ChatGPT / Claude / Gemini logins
  • Fork → clone → edit checkin.json → push
  • Confirm your card turns green on the projector check-in dashboard
Open Lab 1 — Setup & First Run →
Lab 2 · Team of 4 · Feeds your Diamond badge

Build a Transformer Next-Word Predictor

Google Colab · HuggingFace Transformers · Gradio · 8 missions

In teams of 4, load a small language model (SmolLM2 / DistilGPT2 / Qwen2.5), inspect how it tokenises text, and write the code that samples its next-token probabilities. Mission 7 is the one cell you write yourselves — three prompts of your own. Mission 8 wraps it in a live Gradio demo.

  • Load a model, tokenize a prompt, inspect the vocabulary size
  • Read the predict_next_words() function — logits → softmax → top-k
  • Mission 7: write your own 3 prompts, run them, discuss the surprising predictions
  • Mission 8: launch the Gradio demo, copy the gradio.live URL — you'll need it for Lab 3's submission
Team Lead presents for 2 minutes in the Gradio Showdown — model chosen, most interesting prediction, what logits/softmax/top-k mean Open Lab 2 — Next Word Prediction →
📊
AhaSlides — Checkpoint #2 · After Lab 2 (notebook)
Code-reading questions on the Next Word Prediction notebook — tokenization, logits, softmax, top-k, temperature · questions in ahaquizday1.md
Lab 3 · Individual · Scored

AI Trust Audit

Role: Junior AI Quality Analyst, Samsung AI CoE

Not a "which chatbot sounds nicer" comparison — 3 real investigations across ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini Pro, each designed to expose a different way AI confidently gets things wrong, ending in your own synthesized findings report.

  • Investigation 1 — Technical fact hallucination (Galaxy S24 spec deep-dive, fact-check every claim)
  • Investigation 2 — Fabricated citation hallucination (ask for a specific source, verify it actually exists)
  • Investigation 3 — Bias swap test (identical candidate profile, only the name changes — does the AI's judgment change too?)
  • Write a 3–5 sentence synthesis: would you trust these tools, unsupervised, for a customer-facing or hiring-related task?
Stretch: design your own 4th investigation testing privacy, copyright, or safety — feeds your Diamond badge Open Lab 3 — AI Trust Audit →
Submitting — one push, at the end of the day

Lab 2 and Lab 3 both feed into a single file. After finishing Lab 3, copy the JSON template at the bottom of Lab 3, fill in both your Lab 2 fields (team, model used, Gradio URL) and your Lab 3 fields (all 3 investigations + your synthesis), save it as submissions/day1.json, and push once. Full steps are in day1readme.md.

git add submissions/day1.json
git commit -m "Day 1 submission - YOUR NAME"
git push origin main
